question: Based on this image, what is the most likely diagnosis?

options.A: Mycotic pseudoaneurysm of right common iliac artery

ground_truth_reasoning:
First, I examine the coronal contrast-enhanced CT image for vascular abnormalities. I identify a focal, contrast-filled saccular outpouching arising from the right common iliac artery region; the lesion is lobulated with small daughter lobulations and has irregular walls. Around this focal outpouching there is adjacent soft-tissue density and small locules of gas/air, suggesting an associated infective or inflammatory process involving the vessel wall.

Next, I relate these image findings to the diagnostic question, which asks for the most likely diagnosis among vascular and retroperitoneal options. The presence of a saccular, irregular, contrast-opacified outpouching arising from the right common iliac artery strongly favors a pseudoaneurysm of that artery. The surrounding soft-tissue change and gas make an infected (mycotic) etiology more likely rather than a simple degenerative aneurysm.

Then I consider the other options in light of the imaging: a saccular abdominal aortic aneurysm would arise from the aorta itself, but the abnormality here is clearly at the right common iliac artery, not the aortic lumen. A retroperitoneal abscess could explain surrounding collection and gas but does not account for the contrast-filled arterial outpouching as the primary lesion. An iliac arteriovenous fistula would be expected to show direct communication or early opacification of adjacent veins, which is not seen on this image.

Finally, synthesizing these observations, the image most consistently demonstrates an infected (mycotic) pseudoaneurysm of the right common iliac artery given the lobulated contrast-filled outpouching at the RCIA and adjacent inflammatory/air-containing changes.
sub_caption: (D) Coronal section shows irregular outpouching (arrows) from the right common iliac artery (RCIA) suggestive of mycotic pseudoaneurysm (MPA) with small daughter blebs (small arrows).
relevant_image_context: A 66-year-old male was admitted to our emergency department with recurrent low-grade fever for 20 days in addition to bilateral leg swelling, progressive generalized weakness, and loss of appetite. The plain CT abdomen showed a large, retroperitoneal collection with air foci at the level of the aortic bifurcation and around the proximal common iliac vessels. Erosion of the IVC walls with luminal extension of air foci was evident even on plain CT. The CECT confirmed a large complex mycotic pseudoaneurysm (MPA) arising from the proximal right common iliac artery (RCIA) with a contained leak. The aneurysm and the associated inflammatory changes clearly eroded the adjacent caval walls with significant wall thickening and enhancement—representing phlebitis. A mixture of air and thrombus is seen distending the long column of infrarenal IVC—indicating septic or suppurative thrombus. Paravertebral, psoas muscle abscesses, and early abdominal wall collateral veins were also visualized. Note is made of atherosclerotic plaques in the aorta and common iliac arteries. In our case, complicated by caval thrombus, the IVC wall enhancement (enhancing venous wall sign) indicates phlebitis; the air pockets within the caval thrombus (thrombus with air inclusion sign) reflect septic thrombus. To our knowledge, similar presentations are seldom reported in literature. Definitive treatment for MPA includes open resection of infected arterial segment, local debridement, in situ reconstructions, extra-anatomic bypass grafts, or endovascular stenting. Caval thrombosis is treated by thrombolysis, thrombectomy, or segmental excision.
